title: Isaiah 31
subtitle: Woe to Those Who Go Down to Egypt

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “The Egyptians Are Mortals, Not God; Their Horses Are Flesh, Not Spirit”

heb.paragraph: "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, who rely on horses. But the Egyptians are mortals (adām), not God (ʾēl); their horses are flesh (bāśār), not spirit (rūaḥ)" (v.3). The sharpest formulation of the Egypt-trust critique. Two contrasts: mortal vs God, flesh vs spirit. "Like birds hovering overhead, the LORD Almighty will shield Jerusalem; he will shield it and deliver it, he will pass over it and will rescue it" (v.5). The “passing over” echoes Passover (Ex 12). God as mother-bird: hovering, protecting.

notes.note: "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, who rely on horses." The fourth time Isaiah condemns the Egypt-alliance. Horses = military technology. Trusting technology instead of theology.
"The Egyptians are mortals, not God; their horses are flesh, not spirit." The binary that defines Isaiah’s theology: flesh vs spirit, mortal vs divine. All political calculation reduces to this.
"Like birds hovering overhead, the LORD will shield Jerusalem; he will pass over and rescue it." The Passover verb: pāsaḥ — pass over. God as mother-bird: wings spread, hovering, protecting.

refs.note: No king saved by the size of his army, no warrior by great strength — a horse is vain hope.
The king must not acquire many horses from Egypt — Torah's anti-Egypt-alliance stance.
'Assyria cannot save us; we will not mount warhorses' — the same trust theology.
'Not by might nor by power, but by my Spirit' — the prophetic consensus against military trust.

hist.context: Chapter 31 parallels chapter 30: another woe against the Egyptian alliance. Egypt's horses are 'flesh, not spirit' (v.3) — a fundamental category distinction. The lion simile (v.4) is striking: Yahweh will not be scared off by shepherds (Assyria) but will fight for Zion. Yet he also fights 'against' it — this dual aspect (judgment and deliverance) characterizes Isaiah's view. The call to 'return' (v.6) is an invitation still open despite the woes.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt emphasizes the flesh/spirit contrast: horses are merely flesh; Yahweh is spirit. Trusting in flesh guarantees failure. This isn't anti-military per se but anti-trust-in-military.
The dual images (lion, birds) show Yahweh's dual action: fierce against enemies, protective of Zion. 'Passing over' (v.5) echoes Passover — Yahweh protects Jerusalem as he protected Israel in Egypt.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs notes the chapter's tight structure: woe (vv.1-3), salvation announcement (vv.4-5), call to repentance (vv.6-7), Assyria's fall (vv.8-9). The woe leads through to hope; judgment is not final.
Assyria falls 'by a sword, not of mortals' — divine action, not human victory. Childs sees this confirmed in the Sennacherib narrative (chs.36-37) where the angel of the LORD strikes the army.

sections.header: Verses 6–9 — “Return to the One You Have So Greatly Revolted Against”

heb.paragraph: "Return (shūbū), you Israelites, to the One your hands have so greatly revolted against" (v.6). The call to return — the imperative form of the verb that gives ch.30:15 its theology. Throw away your idols of silver and gold (v.7). Assyria will fall by a sword not of mortals (v.8) — "not by human" echoes Daniel 2:34 (the stone cut without hands) and 8:25 (destroyed not by human power). God’s furnace is in Zion (v.9). The fire is his, in Jerusalem.

notes.note: "Return to the One you have revolted against. In that day each of you will reject the idols of silver and gold your sinful hands have made." Repentance produces idol-disposal. You cannot return to God and keep the alternatives.
"Assyria will fall by a sword that is not of man. His stronghold will fall because of terror." Not-by-man: the divine pattern. God’s defeats of empires require no human army.

refs.note: The angel of the LORD strikes 185,000 Assyrians — the fulfillment of 31:8's prophecy.
The parallel account of Sennacherib's army's destruction.
Gog's destruction by divine intervention follows the same pattern — human armies irrelevant.

hist.context: The 'idols of silver and gold' (v.7) that Israel must throw away are the very idols of Egypt they trusted. The journey to Egypt for help involved adopting Egyptian religious practices. Isaiah demands a clean break: return to Yahweh AND abandon the idols. The 'rock' (v.9) that Assyria's princes flee to is probably a term for their god — who will fail them. Assyria's 'fire in Zion' is the altar fire that consumes their arrogance.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the connection between political alliance and religious syncretism. Going to Egypt meant adopting Egyptian ways. The call to return includes purging foreign religious elements.
The 'not human' sword echoes 30:27-33's theophany. Oswalt sees this as one of Isaiah's clearest statements: salvation comes from Yahweh alone, not human strategy.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the return-and-idol-purge command as demanding total reorientation. Political trust in Egypt and religious trust in idols are one syndrome. True return addresses both dimensions simultaneously. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: "Like birds hovering overhead, the LORD will shield Jerusalem…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVFor in that day every one of you will reject the idols of silver and gold your sinful hands have made.ESVFor in that day everyone shall cast away his idols of silver and his idols of gold, which your hands have sinfully made for you.NIVTheir stronghold will fall because of terror; at the sight of the battle standard their commanders will panic,” declares the Lord, whose fire is in Zion, whose furnace is in Jerusalem.ESVHis rock shall pass away in terror, and his officers desert the standard in panic,” declares the LORD, whose fire is in Zion, and whose furnace is in Jerusalem.

rec.text: ""Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, who rely on horses." The fourth time Isaiah condemns the Egypt-alliance. Horses = military technology. Trusting technology instead of theology."

rows.label: Return! — 31:6–9
rows.text: "RETURN to the One you revolted against." Throw away idols. "Assyria will fall by a sword NOT OF MAN."

hebtext.note: "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, who rely on horses. But the Egyptians are mortals (adām), not God (ʾēl); their horses are flesh (bāśār), not spirit (rūaḥ)" (v.3). The sharpest formulation of the Egypt-trust critique. Two contrasts: mortal vs God, flesh vs spirit. "Like birds hovering overhead, the LORD Almighty will shield Jerusalem; he will shield it and deliver it, he will pass over it and will rescue it" (v.5). The “passing over” echoes Passover (Ex 12). God as mother-bird: hovering, protecting.

"Return (shūbū), you Israelites, to the One your hands have so greatly revolted against" (v.6). The call to return — the imperative form of the verb that gives ch.30:15 its theology. Throw away your idols of silver and gold (v.7). Assyria will fall by a sword not of mortals (v.8) — "not by human" echoes Daniel 2:34 (the stone cut without hands) and 8:25 (destroyed not by human power). God’s furnace is in Zion (v.9). The fire is his, in Jerusalem.

themes.note: Faith peaks: flesh vs spirit, the fundamental binary. Sovereignty: Assyria falls by no human sword. The Passover verb applied to Jerusalem’s rescue.

coaching.tip: 'The Egyptians are mortals, not God; their horses are flesh, not spirit' (v. 3). Isaiah draws the fundamental biblical distinction: Creator versus creature. Egypt's military power isn't evil — it's just finite. The problem with trusting horses isn't that horses are bad but that they're not God. Isaiah's critique of political alliances is ultimately a critique of category confusion.

'Like birds hovering overhead, the LORD Almighty will shield Jerusalem' (v. 5). The image of God as a protective bird appears in Deuteronomy 32:11 (eagle), Psalm 91:4 (wings), and Jesus's lament: 'how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks' (Matthew 23:37). The metaphor evolves — from soaring eagle to hovering guardian to grieving hen — tracking God's relationship with Israel.
